mnemonics.title: Hazardous E-Waste Components
mnemonics.content: Remember hazardous e-waste substances using: 'Lead Mercury Cadmium = LeMeCaD' - Lethal Metals Causing Damage

mnemonics.title: EPR Responsibilities
mnemonics.content: EPR = Producers must 'COLLECT' - Create collection centers, Obtain authorization, List targets, Ensure recycling, File returns, Transmit to authorities

mnemonics.title: E-Waste Management Steps
mnemonics.content: Steps: 'DCRR' - Dismantle, Collect hazardous, Recover materials, Recycle properly

acronyms.usage: Policy making producers responsible for end-of-life management of their products
National body coordinating e-waste management implementation with SPCBs
State-level body granting authorizations to e-waste handlers and monitoring compliance
Discarded electrical or electronic devices at end of useful life
Flame retardant chemicals in electronics that are persistent organic pollutants

visualTips.visualization: Think of a pyramid: Top = Prevention/Reduction, then Reuse, then Recycling, then Energy Recovery, Bottom = Disposal (least preferred)
Draw arrow flow: Producer → Consumer → Collection Center → Dismantler → Recycler → Producer (cycle of responsibility)
CRT monitors contain lead, Batteries contain cadmium/mercury, Circuit boards contain lead/tin, Cables contain PVC/lead

analogies.analogy: Like a doctor's referral system - consumers (patients) bring waste to collection centers (clinics) who refer to specialists (dismantlers/recyclers)

analogies.analization: Like a 'take-back' policy in bottle deposits - manufacturers accept old products when selling new ones

analogies.analogy: Urban mining - e-waste is like a mine with precious metals (gold, copper) that can be extracted and reused
